title: Electrospray ionization of neutral metal dithiocarbamate complexes using in-source oxidation
abstract:
JOURNAL OF MASS SPECTROMETRY
J. Mass Spectrom. 34, 1069вҖ“1078 (1999)
Electrospray Ionization of Neutral Metal
Dithiocarbamate Complexes Using In-source
Oxidation
Dale F. Schoener,1вҖ  Mark A. Olsen,2 Paul G. Cummings2 and Cecilia Basic1*вҖЎ
1 Department of Chemistry, Villanova University, 800 Lancaster Avenue, Villanova, Pennsylvania 19085, USA
2 SmithKline Beecham Pharmaceuticals, King of Prussia, Pennsylvania 19046, USA
An electrospray ionization (ESI) mass spectral characterization of eight substituted tris(dithiocarbamato)iron(III)
complexes (Fe(III)(dtc)3) and of the diethyldithiocarbamate (et2dtc)вҲ’, complexes of Mn(III), Cu(II), Ni(II), and
Co(III) is presented. Use is made of the inherent electrochemical potential of the ESI source to oxidize the neutral
metal complexes and in-source collision-induced dissociation (CID) to characterize the resulting ions. The mass
spectra obtained at low source offset voltages all show molecular ions of the oxidized complexes, in addition to
ions due to the loss of a dithiocarbamate, (dtc)вҲ’, ligand. Three of the Fe(III)(dtc)3 complexes also show ions due to
fragmentation of the (dtc)вҲ’ligands. Ions corresponding to the thiuram disulп¬Ғde formed following the oxidation
of excess (dtc)вҲ’in solution are also seen. In-source CID studies of the Fe(III), Mn(III), Cu(II), Ni(II) and Co(III)
(et2dtc)вҲ’complexes all show [M(I)(et2dtc) Y H]Y ions attributed to proton transfer from a protonated solvent
ion to a M(I)(et2dtc) neutral formed upon following CID in the high-pressure region of the ion source. The
results of the present in-source oxidation studies are compared with those obtained in previous ESI studies in
which external oxidizing agents were added to the metal complex solutions prior to analysis. Copyright пӣҷ1999
John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: electrospray; mass spectrometry; metal dithiocarbamates
INTRODUCTION
In recent years, electrospray ionization mass spectrome-
try (ESI-MS) has seen increased use in the analysis and
characterization of gas-phase metal complexes and their
solution chemistries.1,2 Examples include the analysis of
Fe(II), Ru(II) and Os(II) metallocenes,3 Mg(II), Ni(II),
Cu(II), and Zn(II) octaethylporphyrins,4 copper bipyridyl
complexes,5 sodium and potassium crown ethers,6 (LвҖ“L)
Cu(I)(PPh3)C copper mixed phosphine complexes (where
LвҖ“L D 1,10-phenanthroline or a substituted 1,10-phenan-
throline),7 Hg(II)в–·PR3в—Ғ2X2 mercury phosphine complexes
(where R D tri-m- and tri-p-tolylphosphine, tri-o-metho-
xyphenylphosphine, tricyclohexylphosphine and tri-p-
chlorophenylphosphine and X D ClO4� or CF3COO�),8
Zn(II)(DTP)2 complexes (where DTP D dialkyldithiopho-
sphate)9 and (Ind)Re(IV)(CO)3 and (Cp)Re(IV)(CO)3
rhenium complexes (where Ind D indenyl and Cp D
cyclopentadienyl).10
* Correspondence to: C. Basic, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California
Institute of Technology, 4800 Oak Grove Drive, Pasadena, California
91109-8099, USA.
E-mail: cbasic@jpl.nasa.gov
вҖ  Present address, DuPont Pharmaceutical, Stine-Haskell Research
Center, Elkton Road, Newark, Delaware 19714, USA.
вҖЎ Present address: Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of
Technology, 4800 Oak Grove Drive, Pasadena, California 91109-
8099, USA.

tris(dithiocarbamato)iron(III)
complexes

stemmed

our
need to understand better suspected oxidation prod-
ucts seen in the positive ESI mass spectrum of a
bis(dithiocarbamato)nitrosyliron(II)
complex,
Fe(II)NO
(dtc)2, where (dtc)� D N-methyl-D-glucaminedithiocarba-
mate (MGD).11 The Fe(II)NO(MGD)2 complex was one
of three Fe(II)NO(dtc)2 complexes of interest in a pro-
posed method to detect biological nitric oxide (NO) using
ESI-MS.11 In the present study, we made use of the inher-
ent electrochemical potential of the ESI source3,4,12,13
to oxidize the neutral metal complexes and in-source
collision-induced dissociation (CID)14 to characterize the
resulting ions. Bond and co-workers have also used ESI-
MS to characterize a series of neutral Fe(III), Cu(II),
Ni(II), and Co(III) complexes.15вҖ“17 However, in their
studies the external oxidizing agents nitrosonium tetraп¬Ӯu-
oroborate в–·NOBF4в—Ғ15 and AgNO3
16 were added to the
metal complex solutions prior to infusion into the mass
spectrometer, or the oxidation products were formed by
coupling either an on-line or an off-line electrochemical
cell to the ESI source.17 The results of these external oxi-
dation studies will be compared with those obtained in
the present study using in-source oxidation of the neutral
metal complexes.

EXPERIMENTAL
Metal complexes
The Fe(III)(dtc)3 complexes were synthesized by com-
bining aqueous solutions of FeCl3 and the respective
(dtc)� ligand in a 1 : 3 molar ratio.18,19 Sodium salts of
piperidinedithiocarbamate and 1-methylpiperazinedithio-
carbamate were prepared according to Preti and Tosi.20
Synthesis
of
sodium
diisopropyldithiocarbamate
was
based on Uhlin and Akerstrom,21 that of sodium di-n-
butyldithiocarbamate was based on Uhlin and Akerstrom21
and Bailey et al.22 and sodium dicyclohexyldithiocarba-
mate was prepared according to Hall and Tiekink.23 Pyrro-
lidinedithiocarbamate (97%) and dibenzyldithiocarbamate
(97%) were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI,
USA). The preparation of Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 was based
on Hendrickson et al.24 and Cu(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 was pre-
pared according to Thorn and Ludwig.18 The synthe-
sis of Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 was based on Hendrickson, et al.25
and that of Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 was based on Bond et al.26
The purity of the ligands and complexes was conп¬Ғrmed
using thin-layer chromatography and the structures of
the piperidinedithiocarbamate, methylpiperazinedithiocar-
bamate and sodium di-n-butyldithiocarbamate and the
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes were con-
п¬Ғrmed using NMR spectroscopy.11
Mass spectrometry
MS analyses of the complexes, with the exception of
the Fe(III)в–·bz2dtcв—Ғ3 and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes, were
performed on a Vestec ESI E200 single-quadrupole mass
spectrometer. Solutions of 0.1 mM of the complexes
were prepared in 50 : 50 acetonitrileвҖ“acetone (HPLC
grade, Baker, Phillipsburg, NJ, and Spectrum Gardena,
CA, USA) containing 0.1 mM lithium triп¬Ӯate (96%,
Aldrich).28вҖ“30 Samples were infused at a п¬Ӯow-rate of
4 Вөl min�1. The ESI needle was held at 2.25вҖ“2.40 kV,
the nozzle at 100 V and the collimator at 10 V. Spectra
of the complexes obtained on the Vestec MS with no
in-source CID are the average of three background-
subtracted spectra. In-source CID studies were performed
on the Vestec E200 by increasing the repeller voltage
to provide offset voltages (пңұV) of 0вҖ“48 V between the
repeller and the collimator. CID spectra were acquired at
a variety of offset voltages, with representative spectra

Dale F. Schoener,1вҖ  Mark A. Olsen,2 Paul G. Cummings2 and Cecilia Basic1*вҖЎ

An electrospray ionization (ESI) mass spectral characterization of eight substituted tris(dithiocarbamato)iron(III)
complexes (Fe(III)(dtc)3) and of the diethyldithiocarbamate (et2dtc)вҲ’, complexes of Mn(III), Cu(II), Ni(II), and
Co(III) is presented. Use is made of the inherent electrochemical potential of the ESI source to oxidize the neutral
metal complexes and in-source collision-induced dissociation (CID) to characterize the resulting ions. The mass

fragmentation of the (dtc)вҲ’ligands. Ions corresponding to the thiuram disulп¬Ғde formed following the oxidation
of excess (dtc)вҲ’in solution are also seen. In-source CID studies of the Fe(III), Mn(III), Cu(II), Ni(II) and Co(III)
(et2dtc)вҲ’complexes all show [M(I)(et2dtc) Y H]Y ions attributed to proton transfer from a protonated solvent
ion to a M(I)(et2dtc) neutral formed upon following CID in the high-pressure region of the ion source. The
results of the present in-source oxidation studies are compared with those obtained in previous ESI studies in
which external oxidizing agents were added to the metal complex solutions prior to analysis. Copyright пӣҷ1999

bining aqueous solutions of FeCl3 and the respective

based on Uhlin and Akerstrom,21 that of sodium di-n-

and Bailey et al.22 and sodium dicyclohexyldithiocarba-
mate was prepared according to Hall and Tiekink.23 Pyrro-
lidinedithiocarbamate (97%) and dibenzyldithiocarbamate
(97%) were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI,
USA). The preparation of Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 was based
on Hendrickson et al.24 and Cu(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 was pre-
pared according to Thorn and Ludwig.18 The synthe-
sis of Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 was based on Hendrickson, et al.25
and that of Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 was based on Bond et al.26
The purity of the ligands and complexes was conп¬Ғrmed
using thin-layer chromatography and the structures of
the piperidinedithiocarbamate, methylpiperazinedithiocar-
bamate and sodium di-n-butyldithiocarbamate and the
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes were con-
п¬Ғrmed using NMR spectroscopy.11
MS analyses of the complexes, with the exception of
the Fe(III)в–·bz2dtcв—Ғ3 and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes, were
performed on a Vestec ESI E200 single-quadrupole mass
spectrometer. Solutions of 0.1 mM of the complexes
were prepared in 50 : 50 acetonitrileвҖ“acetone (HPLC
grade, Baker, Phillipsburg, NJ, and Spectrum Gardena,
CA, USA) containing 0.1 mM lithium triп¬Ӯate (96%,
Aldrich).28вҖ“30 Samples were infused at a п¬Ӯow-rate of
4 Вөl min�1. The ESI needle was held at 2.25вҖ“2.40 kV,
the nozzle at 100 V and the collimator at 10 V. Spectra
of the complexes obtained on the Vestec MS with no
in-source CID are the average of three background-
subtracted spectra. In-source CID studies were performed
on the Vestec E200 by increasing the repeller voltage
to provide offset voltages (пңұV) of 0вҖ“48 V between the
repeller and the collimator. CID spectra were acquired at
a variety of offset voltages, with representative spectra
Copyright пӣҷ1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
(the result of a single background-subtracted acquisition)
presented below. MS analyses of the Fe(III)в–·bz2dtcв—Ғ3
and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes were performed on a
Hewlett-Packard ESI HP5989B single-quadrupole mass
spectrometer. Samples were prepared as above and infused
at 20 Вөl min�1. The ESI source voltages were Vcap D
�4000 V, Vend D �3000 V and Vcyl D �6000 V. In-
source CID was performed by varying the capillary exit
voltage to give пңұV D 80вҖ“280 V. Again, spectra were
acquired at a variety of offset voltages, with representative
spectra presented below. Spectra of the Fe(III)в–·bz2dtcв—Ғ3
and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes are the result of a single
background-subtracted acquisition.
Metal and sulfur ion isotopic distributions were used in
assigning the ion structures in all of the studies presented
below. For simplicity, however, the ions will be discussed
in terms of the m/z ratio of the most abundant isotope.
The ESI mass spectrum of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 obtained at
a пңұV D 10 V is presented in Fig. 1(a); principal ions
in the spectra of the remaining Fe(III)(dtc)3 complexes
obtained at a пңұV D 10 V are presented in Table 3. The
C molecular ions are present in all eight spec-
C fragment ions attributed to the
loss of a (dtc)� ligand. Since no oxidizing agents were
present in the solution prior to analysis, formation of
C ions is attributed to in-source oxidation
occurring during the ESI process.3,4,12,13 The half-wave
oxidation potentials (E1/2) of the Fe(III)(dtc)3 complexes
(0.41вҖ“0.66 V31) are below the 1.3 V value assigned to the
upper range of the effective electrochemical potential of
the ESI source.3,4 Observation of the Fe(IV)(dtc)3
is facilitated by the fact that (dtc)� ligands are known to
stabilize unusually high metal oxidation states.32,33 Both
the ESI mass spectra of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3, Fe(IIIв—Ғв–·pr2dtcв—Ғ3,
Figure 1. ESI mass spectra of Fe(III)(et2dtc)3 obtained in 50 : 50 acetonitrile acetone containing 0.1 mM lithium triflate. (a) пңұV D 10 V;
(b) пңұV D 38 V. Et2dtc D diethyldithiocarbamate; et4tds D tetraethylthiuram disulfide.
Copyright пӣҷ1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Table 3. Principal ions in the ESI mass spectra of the
a All spectra were acquired on a Vestec ESI E200 mass
spectrometer at a пңұV of 10 V except for that of Fe(III)(bz2dtc)3,
which was acquired on a Hewlett Packard HP5989B at a пңұV of
Fe(III)(morphdtc)3 (where morphdtc D morpholinedithio-
carbamate) obtained after the addition of NOBF4 to the
sample solution,15 with the abundance of the Fe(III)(dtc)2
ions increasing upon in-source CID. Low-abundance ions
corresponding to reaction of the complexes and (dtc)� lig-
ands with NOC and NO arising from NOBF4 were also
reported.15 Fe(IV)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3C ions were also seen in the
ESI spectrum of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 obtained after addition
In-source CID studies of the Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 (Fig. 1(b),
пңұV D 38 V) and the Fe(III)в–·n-bu2dtcв—Ғ3 (data not shown)
complexes show that the abundances of the Fe(IV)(dtc)3
ions decrease whereas the Fe(III)(dtc)2
remain as the base peak or their abundances increase upon
CID, indicating that the Fe(III)(dtc)2
and (b) and in Table 3 likely arise from fragmentation of
C ion via a single-electron ligand-to-metal
transfer. Similar M(III)(dtc)2
metalв—Ғare seen in the ESI mass spectra of Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3
[Fig. 2(a) and (b)] and Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 [Fig. 5(a) and (b)]
presented below. Ligand-to-metal electron transfer upon
CID has previously been reported for Mn(II) and Co(II)
acetonitrile complexes,34 for [Cu(II) (DMSO)3]2C 35,36
and for [Fe(III)в–·ox�в—Ғ3 C H]C.37
The ions at m/z 116 in the spectra of Fe(III)в–·et2dtc)3
presented in Fig. 1(a) and (b) are assigned to (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC;
(dtc-Sв—ҒC ions are also seen in the spectra of the other
Fe(III)в–·dtcв—Ғ3 complexes, with the exception of Fe(III)
(c-hx2dtc)3 and Fe(III)в–·bz2dtcв—Ғ3 (Table 3). No в–·dtc-Sв—ҒC
ions were reported in the studies of Bond and co-
workers.15,16 Two possible mechanisms for the formation
of the (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC ions upon in-source CID are presented
in Schemes 1(a)38 and (b).39 In Scheme 1(a),38 the corre-
sponding M(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2C ion undergoes a two-electron
ligand-to-metal transfer to form a neutral M(I)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ
complex and a transient (et2dtc)C ion which then loses
S to form (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC, similar to the mechanism proposed
for the fragmentation of the dimethyl (dtcв—Ғ� complex of
Zn(II) following electron ionization (EI).38 Alternatively
(Scheme 1(b)39), the M(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2C ion may fragment
to form an M(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC ion and a neutral (et2dtc) via a
single-electron transfer, with the (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC ion formed
from the subsequent loss of M(I)S from the M(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC
ion, similar to the mechanism proposed for the frag-
mentation of Ti(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 complexes following EI.39
M(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC ions (Scheme 1(b)39) are not seen in the
ESI spectra of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 or Cu(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2; how-
ever, they are seen in the spectra of Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3
[Fig. 2(b)], Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 [Fig. 4(a) and (b)] and Co(III)
в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 [Fig. 5(b)]. The ion at m/z 205 in Fig. 1(b) is
assigned to [Fe(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C; similar [M(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC
H]C ions are seen in the CID spectra of Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3
below. Bond et al.15 did not report these [M(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC
H]C ions in any of their spectra. The [M(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C
ions seen here may arise from proton transfer from a
solvent ion (e.g. protonated acetone seen in the back-
ground) to the neutral M(I)в–·et2dtcв—Ғcomplex proposed in
Scheme 1(a)38 in the CID region of the mass spectrometer.
It is interesting that [M(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C ions have also
been reported in the EI mass spectra of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3,39
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ240 and the morpholine в–·dtcв—Ғ� complexes of
Fe(III) and Co(III),41 although no explanations were pro-
vided for their origins. Ions arising from proton transfer
reactions were reported in the EI spectra of a series of
alkyl dithiocarbamate sodium salts.38 The authors pro-
posed that evaporation of the hydrated sodium salts
provided a ready proton source.38 The origin of the
[M(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C ions seen in Fig. 1(b)вҖ“5(b) warrants
further study, especially that of Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 which
forms the [Fe(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C ion in the uncommon (I)
oxidation state but does not form a M(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC ion in
the more common (II) oxidation state. (For Mn, Cu and
Co, (I) is a common oxidation state. (I) is an uncommon
oxidation state for Ni; however, unlike Fe(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3,
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 also forms ions in the more common (II)
oxidation state as seen in Fig. 4(a) and (b)).

Figure 2(a) and (b) present the ESI mass spectra of

tively. At a пңұV of 10 V, the Mn(IV)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3C molecular

is seen at m/z 303 as is the (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC ion at m/z 116. At
a пңұV of 48 V [Fig. 2(b)], the intensity of the (et2dtc-Sв—ҒC
ion increases and the [Mn(I)в–·et2dtc) C H]C (m/z 204) and
Mn(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC (m/z 203) ions appear. The ions at m/z
280 and 274 in Fig. 2(b) are attributed to fragmentation
of the в–·et2dtcв—Ғ� ligand of the Mn(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2C ion, with
the m/z 280 ion corresponding to the loss of C4H9N and
the m/z 274 ion corresponding to the loss of CHS2. (Loss
of CS2 has been reported in the EI mass spectra of a
series of metal dithiocarbamates39 and in the EI spectra
of a series mixed Co(III) (dtc)� complexes.43) The ion at
m/z 260 may be due to loss of C2H3S2 from the в–·et2dtcв—Ғ�
ligand, while that at m/z 236 can be attributed to the loss
of C5H9NS. Further tandem MS studies are required to
conп¬Ғrm these assignments, especially the proposed losses
Figure 3(a) and (b) present the ESI mass spectra of

NOBF415 and upon coupling an electrochemical cell to
the ESI source.17 They also reported a Cu(II)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC
C H]C ion seen here. Note: of the series of complexes
studied by Bond et al.,15 M(II)(dtcв—ҒC ions were seen
only in the spectra of Cu(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2, Cu(II)(me2dtcв—Ғ2
and Cu(II)(pr2dtcв—Ғ2. Formation of the [Cu(I)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ
acetonitrileвҖ“acetoneвҖ“0.1 mM lithium triп¬Ӯate here versus
1 : 10 dichloromethaneвҖ“methanol15). It is not, however,

[Co(I)в–·et2dtcв—ҒC H]C ion in their Co(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3 spectra.
Figure 4(a) and (b) present the ESI mass spectra of
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 obtained at a пңұV of 10 and 28 V, respec-
tively. At a пңұV of 10 V, the Ni(IV)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3C п¬Ғnal oxida-
tion product25,32 is seen at m/z 502. The Ni(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2C

mentation of Ni(IV)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ3C, since the Ni(III)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2C

(et2dtcв—Ғ� complexes, no [et4tds C Li]C ions (m/z 303)
are observed, possibly because when Ni(II)(dtcв—Ғ2 com-

Ni(II)(dtcв—Ғ2 complexes scavenge the (dtc)Еҫ radicals to
produce relatively unstable Ni(III)(dtcв—Ғ3 species.32 The
[Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 C Li]C and [Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 C Na]C mole-
cular ions are seen at m/z 361 and 377, respectively,
while the ion at m/z 477 is attributed to the association of
two acetone molecules with the [в–·Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 C Liв—Ғ]C
ion. A lithium-bound dimer of Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2 is also seen
at m/z 715. The increased abundance of adduct ions,
relative to the other metal (et2dtcв—Ғ� complexes, may
reп¬Ӯect the higher half-wave oxidation potential (E1/2) of
Ni(II)в–·et2dtcв—Ғ2в–·0.89 V25) and the less sterically hindered,
planar geometry of the complex.32
